data.title: Surf Air Mobility outlines 20–30% revenue growth target for 2026 with platform pivot and BETA Technologies partnership
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Surf Air Mobility Inc. (SRFM) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Deanna White stated the company is moving from a stabilization phase into "growth mode," highlighting a 20% to 30% increase in 2026 revenue guidance compared to the prior year. This guidance does not include early electric aircraft deployment, which is expected to contribute to revenue and efficiencies in 2027 through a new partnership with BETA Technologies.
  • White described the year as a "dramatic step change in operational performance," noting, "We achieved our guidance of profitability in our Airline Operations for the full year of 2025, defined as positive adjusted EBITDA." She emphasized strategic initiatives such as the launch of "Powered by Surf On Demand" and "Surf On Demand Cargo," both beginning to generate profitable revenue in 2025.
  • The company raised over $100 million in equity during 2025, which "substantially reduced our overall cost of capital and lower our net debt," providing flexibility to pursue growth.
  • Surf Air Mobility secured a firm order for 25 BETA electric aircraft, with options for 75 more, and reached an agreement to become BETA’s exclusive factory authorized service center in Hawaii. White said, "We anticipate that the improved unit economics of BETA electric aircraft will lead to increased profitability in our scheduled service and on-demand charter businesses over time."
  • Chief Financial Officer Oliver Reeves reported, "Net debt decreased 47% from $139 million at December 31, 2024, to $74 million at December 31, 2025. This decrease in net debt also benefited from the conversion of $48 million in convertible notes, inclusive of interest."

Outlook

  • For 2026, Surf Air Mobility projects revenue in the range of $128 million to $138 million and adjusted EBITDA loss in the range of $40 million to $50 million. CFO Reeves said, "Our revenue guidance range of 20% to 30% year-over-year revenue growth for fiscal year 2026 contemplates accelerating growth in our on-demand charter business and partial year revenue contribution for SurfOS."
  • Revenue growth is expected to be "heavily weighted to the back half of 2026" due to SurfOS commercialization and on-demand charter expansion. The company expects adjusted EBITDA loss and margins to improve each quarter on a comparable year-over-year basis.
  • First quarter 2026 revenue is anticipated to range from $24 million to $26 million, with adjusted EBITDA loss between $15.5 million and $13.5 million.

Financial Results

  • Fourth quarter 2025 revenue was $26.4 million, within guidance, and marked an eighth consecutive quarter meeting or exceeding guidance. This represented a 9% sequential decrease, attributed to the exit of unprofitable routes, offset by an 8% increase in on-demand charter revenue.
  • Full year 2025 revenue totaled $106.6 million, an 11% decrease from 2024, primarily due to a 15% decline in scheduled service revenue, partially offset by a 3% increase in on-demand charter revenue.
  • Adjusted EBITDA loss for Q4 2025 was just under $8 million, a 19% improvement sequentially. Full year adjusted EBITDA loss improved 5% year-over-year to $41.7 million.
  • Operational improvements included a controllable completion factor of 98% in Q4 2025, compared to 89% in Q4 2024. On-time departures and arrivals also improved year-over-year.

Q&A

  • Amit Dayal, H.C. Wainwright: Asked about SurfOS investment and rollout. CEO White explained the go-to-market strategy and noted, "The bulk of that revenue that we're going to get this year from commercializing SurfOS is going to occur in the second half of the year."
  • Dayal inquired about the BETA partnership’s revenue impact. White clarified, "We cannot take deliveries and put them into full commercial service until the aircraft is certified, but we have plans to start with the CTOL cargo and move to the passenger variant."
  • Dayal sought details on BETA aircraft economics. White stated, "We anticipate that there'll be 30% more improvement in operating costs. Where that will come from is 2 areas: fuel and also maintenance."
  • Dayal asked about the balance sheet post-investment. CFO Reeves responded, "We believe these investments will generate significant ROI, and it will create shareholder value."
  • David Storms, Stonegate: Queried Airline Operations profitability drivers. White said technology and electric aircraft adoption are key, with a "goal to become a premier MRO for the electric aircraft."
  • Storms probed BETA partnership geographic expansion. White declined specifics for competitive reasons but emphasized the advantage of an existing network for rapid adoption.
  • Storms asked about early traction in Surf On Demand. White reported a "big uptick" and a pipeline of independent brokers for the program.
  • Austin Moeller, Canaccord: Asked about timing for BrokerOS and SurfOS revenue. White projected first half of 2026, with more significant revenue in the second half.
  • Moeller inquired about Caravan Electrification. Co-Founder Sudhin Shahani confirmed continued pursuit of partnerships, with no intention to self-fund the program.
  • Investor-submitted questions touched on topics including the shift from in-house electrification, technical certification hurdles for electric aircraft, revenue segment profitability, and SurfOS integration with OEMs and eVTOL manufacturers. Management confirmed ongoing discussions with multiple industry stakeholders.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts maintained a positive tone, expressing congratulatory remarks and focusing on growth areas, partnerships, and operational gains. Questions were largely constructive, seeking clarity on execution timelines and economic impact.
  • Management exhibited confidence and strategic clarity, repeatedly referencing "growth mode," "pivoting to growth," and "significant investment priorities." Phrases like "We anticipate" and "We believe" signaled optimism tempered by acknowledgment of certification dependencies.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both analysts and management sustained a positive to slightly more optimistic sentiment, with greater emphasis on growth and platform transition.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language shifted from stabilization and transformation to "pivoting to growth" and a platform-centric strategy.
  • The introduction of a 20% to 30% revenue growth target for 2026, a substantial increase from previous guidance, was a notable change.
  • Management’s focus shifted from capital structure optimization and cost control to scaling partnerships, platform deployment, and electric aircraft adoption.
  • Analysts’ questions evolved from concerns about route exits and cost reductions to inquiries about SurfOS commercialization, electric aircraft economics, and growth drivers.
  • Management tone moved from cautious optimism to more assertive confidence regarding execution and path to profitability.

Risks and Concerns

  • Key challenges cited included certification timelines for electric aircraft, which remain the "largest hurdle" before commercial flights.
  • Management no longer plans to invest $50 million to $100 million in the Caravan Electrification program, instead pursuing partnerships to reduce capital risk.
  • The company is making "significant investments" in SurfOS and Hawaii, reflected in projected adjusted EBITDA loss for 2026.
  • Exposure to fuel price volatility is mitigated by operating a fuel-efficient fleet, with future benefits anticipated from electric aircraft.

Final Takeaway

Surf Air Mobility’s Q4 2025 call underscores a decisive transition from financial and operational stabilization to aggressive growth, with a 2026 revenue target reflecting 20% to 30% anticipated expansion. The company’s platform-centric strategy, underpinned by proprietary software, expanded on-demand charter programs, and a strategic partnership with BETA Technologies, positions it to capitalize on the next wave of aviation innovation. Management’s focus on operational efficiency, new revenue streams such as MRO services, and disciplined investment in digital infrastructure signals a commitment to capturing a growing share of the advanced air mobility ecosystem in the years ahead.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Japan’s energy import shock could ripple into yen carry trade and U.S. Treasuries, Pelletier says
data.description:
Rising oil prices could create renewed pressure across global financial markets by straining Japan’s currency, threatening the popular yen carry trade and potentially influencing flows in the U.S. Treasury market, according to Martin Pelletier, portfolio manager at TriVest Wealth Counsel. 

Pelletier on X noted that Japan is particularly vulnerable to energy shocks because it imports nearly all of its fuel. 

“Rising oil prices pose a particular risk for Japan because the country imports nearly all of its energy,” he wrote on X, adding that higher crude costs quickly widen the country’s trade deficit and weigh on the currency.

That dynamic can become self-reinforcing. “High oil prices immediately widen Japan’s trade deficit, putting downward pressure on the yen,” Pelletier said. Because oil is priced in dollars, a weaker Japanese currency raises the local cost of energy, feeding imported inflation through electricity, transportation, and broader consumer prices.

Such pressure could force policy changes from the Bank of Japan, potentially undermining the economics of the widely used yen carry trade, which relies on ultra-low Japanese interest rates.

Pelletier also highlighted implications for the U.S. Treasury market.

“Japan holds roughly $1.18T–$1.20T in U.S. Treasuries, making it the largest foreign holder,” he wrote, noting that Tokyo previously sold Treasuries in 2022 to fund foreign-exchange intervention when oil surged following the Russian invasion of Ukraine.

data.title: McDonald's to pressure fast-food rivals with $3 and under menu items
data.description:
McDonald's (MCD) continues to put pressure on the rest of the fast-food sector with a new "$3 and under" addition to its value platform that will reportedly begin rolling in the U.S. in April.

Since early 2025, McDonald's (MCD) has used its McValue platform and Extra Value Meals to create a more coherent, national value message after years of relying heavily on local deals and app-only offers. McDonald's (MCD) management has repeatedly framed affordability as part of the company's DNA to position McValue as the everyday value anchor and fight back against the perception that it was no longer a pricing leader, while still supporting premium items like Best Burger and McCafé to balance traffic and check growth.

Under the new structure, specific items like sausage biscuits and four-piece Chicken McNuggets are expected to anchor the under-$3 tier, while a $4 breakfast bundle with a McMuffin, hash browns, and coffee will target morning traffic. The offers are designed to echo the older $1, $2, and $3 dollar menus, and they are meant to compete with low-price platforms at rivals such as Taco Bell's (YUM) value menu and new Burger King (QSR) promotions.

RBC Capital Markets analysts Logan Reich said a bullish view could be the launch improves McDonald's (MCD) value perceptions, particularly among lower-income consumers. "We think this change could just be an optimization of MCD's value offerings, as they have a year+ of learnings following the launch of McValue. Further, the unanimous franchisee support suggests franchisees are on board with the value strategy," he highlighted.

data.title: Boeing repairing 25 737 MAX jets after wiring issue disrupts deliveries - Bloomberg
data.description:
Boeing (BA) +2.6% in Friday's trading after Bloomberg reported the company is repairing damaged wiring in as many as 25 undelivered 737 MAX jets, disrupting near-term deliveries of the aircraft but likely less than initially anticipated.

Boeing (BA) shares slid 3% on March 10, when it reported the latest quality flaw in the 737 MAX without disclosing how many aircraft were affected by the lapse, but the company reportedly has been assuring customers the fallout will be short-lived.

The company has handed over just three 737 MAX jets so far this month, and the last deliveries were March 4, according to data from Cirium Ltd., a sharp drop from February, when the company handed over 43 of the jets.

Investors are closely following the 737 production ramp-up because higher deliveries are crucial to returning the company to better financial footing.

data.title: Mach Natural Resources plans to lower Mancos well costs to $13M in 2026 as drilling pivots to gas
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Mach Natural Resources LP (MNR) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Tom Ward reiterated Mach's four strategic pillars, emphasizing "delivering exceptional cash returns through distributions" and stating the company has distributed $1.3 billion to unitholders since 2018 and $5.67 per unit from the start of 2024 through the last announced distribution. Ward highlighted, "maximizing distributions is the culmination of the other 3 and the most important."
  • Ward described disciplined execution, noting, "Mach has never acquired an asset by paying more than PDP PV-10...We have accomplished this goal 23 times and do not see an end to the requirement." He detailed the company’s ability to buy distressed assets and later benefit from market re-ratings, referencing the Sabinal purchase and the strategy of holding acreage with low cost basis for potential sales.
  • The company recently shifted drilling focus from oil-dominated assets to dry gas locations in the Deep Anadarko and San Juan, citing commodity price dynamics: "In 2026, our drilling is once again concentrating on drilling natural gas wells in the San Juan and Deep Anadarko through the first half of this year. However, we are now preparing to bring back an oil rig in the Oswego and associated oil areas in the last half of 2026 if crude prices remain elevated."
  • Ward highlighted flexibility in shifting capital, stating, "We will plan to reduce the Deep Anadarko CapEx by moving from 2 rigs to 1 rig and bring back on the Oswego program if the market allows."
  • The company targets a reinvestment rate of no more than 50% and aims to slightly grow barrels of oil equivalent in 2026. Ward stated, "Our goal is to lower the drilling and completion cost to approximately $13 million during the 2026 drilling season."
  • CFO Kevin White reported, "2025 year-end reserves capturing the results of 2025 drilling and acquisitions during the year more than doubled from 337 million to 705 million barrels of oil equivalent. Also worth noting the additions from the results of our development program exceeded the 2025 production by 18%."
  • White added, "For the quarter, our production of 154,000 Boe per day was 17% oil, 68% natural gas and 15% NGLs."

Outlook

  • Management anticipates "slightly growing our barrels of oil equivalent while maintaining our desired reinvestment rate" for 2026.
  • The company plans to continue testing the market for acreage sales and may bring an oil rig back in the Oswego program in the second half of 2026 if crude prices stay elevated.
  • Plans are in place to lower the cost of drilling and completing Mancos wells to $13 million, with a 2026 drilling season running from April 1 through November.

Financial Results

  • Year-end reserves more than doubled to 705 million Boe, with development program additions exceeding 2025 production by 18%.
  • Production for the quarter reached 154,000 Boe per day with an average realized price of $58.14 per barrel of oil, $2.54 per Mcf of gas, and $21.28 per barrel of NGLs. Oil and gas revenues totaled $331 million, and total revenues including hedges and midstream activities were $388 million.
  • Lease operating expenses were $106 million, cash G&A was $11 million, and development CapEx was $77 million, representing 46% of operating cash flow. Cash available for distribution was $89 million, and a distribution of $0.53 per unit was paid.

Q&A

  • Neal Dingmann, William Blair: Asked about additional activities to take advantage of higher oil prices. Tom Ward replied, "If our cash flow can move up a bit, we would put more -- maybe a second rig in and out to be bringing on more oil if it's staying in the 70s."
  • Dingmann followed up on the M&A market. Ward responded, "We're pretty much on the sidelines for M&A until we move down our debt...our focus is to pay down debt, and then we might be able to do that, though, by bringing in a partner in the Deep Anadarko."
  • Derrick Whitfield, Texas Capital: Asked about monetizing assets and value parameters. Ward stated, "There are 3 ways that we can bring our debt down...or we could sell some non-EBITDA generating assets. The Deep Anadarko is the only area that's not HBP and has leasehold to have some term on it."
  • Charles Meade, Johnson Rice: Inquired about oil price requirements for bringing back the Oswego rig. Ward said, "I think any time that you have oil above $70, we have rates of return well north of 50%."
  • Michael Scialla, Stephens: Asked about wider gas differentials and the gas macro. Ward explained, "We are seeing widening basis in the Anadarko and the San Juan...I think the same reason that you have warm weather in the West has caused basis to widen."
  • John Freeman, Raymond James: Noted the increase in midstream profit guidance. White clarified, "We're seeing that there's some MOE midstream operating expense being reclassed to GP&T...it does improve midstream operating profit."
  • Jeffrey Grampp, Northland: Sought clarification on current guidance and Oswego rig shift. Ward responded, "It did not."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts focused on optionality for oil drilling, asset monetization, and M&A strategy, with a neutral to slightly positive tone, seeking clarity on capital allocation and operational flexibility.
  • Management maintained a confident tone, emphasizing discipline, flexibility, and cash flow focus. Phrases like, "we are not in any hurry to sell," and "I feel comfortable that they're going to be north of 50% rate of return," reflect steady confidence.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management’s tone remains confident, though slightly more focused on capital discipline and debt reduction. Analyst sentiment continues to seek assurance on execution and optionality.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter reflects a shift from purely gas-focused drilling to a more balanced approach, with plans to potentially bring back oil drilling in the Oswego if prices remain elevated.
  • Guidance language now includes more detailed cost reduction targets for the Mancos wells and emphasizes asset sales as a lever for debt reduction.
  • Key metrics such as production, reserves, and cash available for distribution have all increased significantly from the previous quarter.
  • Management’s confidence remains strong, with greater emphasis on operational flexibility and disciplined reinvestment. Analysts continue to focus on capital allocation and the potential for asset monetization and drilling partnerships.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management identified the need to lower debt levels before reengaging in M&A activity, and highlighted market-driven risks such as commodity price fluctuations and basis widening.
  • The company is testing the market for acreage sales but is not in a hurry to sell, emphasizing patience.
  • Analyst questions indicated concerns about the timing of bringing rigs back online, the impact of wider gas differentials, and the ability to lower well costs as planned.

Final Takeaway

Mach Natural Resources LP remains focused on maximizing unitholder returns through disciplined capital allocation and operational flexibility. With reserves more than doubling year-over-year and robust cash distributions, the company is poised to adjust its drilling mix in response to commodity prices while targeting lower costs, particularly in the Mancos play. Management’s commitment to maintaining a low reinvestment rate, reducing debt, and opportunistically monetizing assets underpins its strategy for sustainable growth and consistent cash flow returns.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Intuitive Surgical falls after reporting cybersecurity incident
data.description:
Shares of Intuitive Surgical (ISRG) dropped to a session low on Friday after the maker of da Vinci robotic surgical systems reported that it is facing a cybersecurity incident involving some of its internal IT applications.

“Intuitive has determined that information from certain internal IT business applications was accessed by an unauthorized third party as the result of a targeted cybersecurity phishing incident,” Intuitive (ISRG) said.

However, the company reassured that its products, including da Vinci, Ion, and digital platforms, remain safe and operational, as the incident did not impact them.

“There has been no impact on our operations or the work we do to support our customers,” the company added.

Intuitive (ISRG) is the second U.S. MedTech firm to face a system breach this week. On Wednesday, Stryker (SYK) shares fell after a cyberattack, blamed on an Iran-linked activist group, disrupted its Microsoft (MSFT)-based systems worldwide.

data.title: Team outlines top line and EBITDA growth ambitions as new CEO takes helm
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Team, Inc. (TISI) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Gary Hill, Chief Executive Officer, opened his first earnings call as CEO by emphasizing his "extraordinary opportunity" to lead Team, highlighting more than 30 years of industry experience and outlining a focus on accelerating top line growth, enhancing efficiency, and reducing costs. He stated, "I see opportunities to expand our wallet share with existing customers and accelerate our growth in end markets, such as aerospace and midstream, and I'm challenging myself and our entire team to accelerate top line growth, enhance efficiency and reduce costs, which should lead to margin and EBITDA growth."
  • Hill committed to maintaining open communication and focusing on continuous improvement, building on Team’s "unique culture, storied history, strong customer relationships and numerous built-in strengths." He reiterated safety as the highest priority for the company.
  • Nelson Haight, Executive VP & CFO, highlighted the company’s recent financial actions: "In March of 2025, we successfully refinanced our capital structure, lowering our blended interest rate by more than 100 basis points and extending our term loan maturities out to 2030. In September 2025, we closed on a $75 million private placement of preferred stock and warrants that helped us to pay down about $67 million of debt."
  • Haight reported, "Our net debt at the end of 2025 was $279 million down, from about $289.6 million at the end of 2024, and we exited 2025 with strong liquidity of $77.4 million." He credited operational improvements for enabling these moves.

Outlook

  • Hill stated that, due to his recent appointment as CEO, "we will not be providing guidance on fiscal year 2026 at this time to allow for a deeper review of our operational performance, market trends and strategic priorities. We will present a more fulsome update that lays out our longer-term plans and objectives and 2026 guidance to the market after the end of the fiscal quarter."
  • The company expects to continue building momentum into 2026, aiming for further growth in top line and adjusted EBITDA.

Financial Results

  • Haight reported, "For the fourth quarter, revenue was up $11.5 million or 5.4% as compared to the prior year period, driven by an 8.9% increase in our Mechanical Services segment and a 1.9% increase in our Inspection and Heat Treating segment."
  • Operating income for the quarter increased by $4.4 million or 200% year-over-year, attributed to a focus on higher margin opportunities and sustainable cost reductions.
  • Adjusted selling, general and administrative expense was lower by $1 million in absolute terms and 150 basis points as a percentage of revenue versus the prior year period.
  • Adjusted EBITDA for the quarter was $16.4 million, nearly $2 million higher year-over-year.
  • For full-year 2025, revenue increased $44 million or 5.2% year-over-year, with Inspection and Heat Treating up 7.5% and Mechanical Services up 2.8%.
  • Full-year operating income rose by $3.9 million or 39%, and adjusted EBITDA reached $60.7 million, a roughly 12% improvement, with margins expanding to almost 7% from 6.4% in 2024.

Q&A

  • NOT_APPLICABLE. No analyst questions or management responses are present in the current transcript.

Sentiment Analysis

  • NOT_APPLICABLE. No analyst commentary or Q&A is present, so no discernible analyst sentiment.
  • Management’s tone in prepared remarks was confident and forward-looking, emphasizing operational improvements and strategic growth. Hill described being "very excited about our future because we have talented employees and differentiated offerings for our customers."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management remains optimistic, but the absence of guidance introduces a cautious note.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter introduced Gary Hill as CEO, signaling a leadership change from Keith Tucker in the prior quarter.
  • Guidance for 2026 was withheld this quarter, whereas the previous quarter included full-year 2025 growth forecasts and margin targets.
  • Both quarters reported revenue and adjusted EBITDA growth, with continued focus on cost discipline and margin expansion.
  • Financial flexibility improved this quarter with net debt reduction and increased liquidity.
  • Management’s confidence in operational improvements and debt reduction continues, but the tone is more reserved regarding future forecasts pending a strategic review.
  • No analyst sentiment or Q&A comparison is possible as no interactive section is available for either quarter.

Risks and Concerns

  • Hill acknowledged the need for continuous improvement and cited challenges in accelerating top line growth, enhancing efficiency, and reducing costs.
  • Haight noted that, "while we still have some work to do, we are now well positioned to accelerate our top line growth and further expand our cash flow generation."
  • There was no explicit mention of specific external risks or detailed mitigation strategies beyond ongoing operational focus.

Final Takeaway

Team, Inc. delivered strong operational and financial results in Q4 and full-year 2025, supported by revenue and margin growth, cost discipline, and improvements in capital structure. With a new CEO at the helm, the company is focused on strategic review and continuous improvement but is withholding formal 2026 guidance until further evaluation. Management remains confident in Team’s ability to build on its recent momentum and unlock future value through operational excellence and financial flexibility.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Bimini outlines RMBS portfolio reduction as it targets $1.6B AUM expansion through Tom Johnson Investment Management acquisition
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Bimini Capital Management (BMNM) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Chairman and CEO Robert Cauley reported that the fixed income markets “experienced a period of calm as 2025 came to a close and we entered 2026,” with “Agency RMBS perform[ing] well during the fourth quarter of 2025 and for the year, generating returns of 1.7% and 8.58% for the quarter and year, respectively.”
  • Cauley highlighted that Orchid Island Capital’s net income reached $103.4 million for the quarter, with its stockholders' equity increasing from $1.086 billion to $3.72 billion. This growth raised Bimini’s Advisory Services revenue to $4.7 million, up from $4.5 million in the previous quarter.
  • Advisory services revenue grew to $16.6 million in 2025, a 30% year-over-year increase from $12.8 million in 2024. Net interest and dividend income from the investment portfolio also increased, moving from $1.52 million in 2024 to $2.45 million in 2025.
  • Cauley announced the reinstitution of a share buyback plan authorizing up to $2.5 million in share repurchases, stating the company can “invest in our stock to the extent we believe is undervalued.”
  • The acquisition of 80% of Tom Johnson Investment Management (TJIM), with $1.6 billion in assets under management, was detailed: “The transaction is expected to close at the beginning of the second quarter of 2026.” The acquisition will be funded from available cash and liquidating most of the Agency RMBS portfolio.
  • Cauley explained, “If the transaction closes as anticipated, the company will continue to operate in Agency RMBS portfolio although the portfolio will initially be much smaller as the capital to fund the acquisition of TJIM will come from available cash and the liquidation of the majority of the Agency RMBS portfolio in place at year-end 2025.”

Outlook

  • Management indicated uncertainty in the economic outlook due to geopolitical developments: “Inflation, which already was already sticky, may move even higher and the economic outlook has become very uncertain.”
  • Management stated that, following the TJIM acquisition, leverage for the portfolio “will be lower than was typically the case prior to the acquisition should it occur.”
  • Cauley said, “Assuming the transaction closes as anticipated, we will use the first quarter of 2026 earnings call to expand on the details of the transaction, how we plan to operate the business going forward and introduce the management of TGIM.”

Financial Results

  • Bimini’s Advisory Services revenue for the fourth quarter was $4.7 million compared to $4.5 million in Q3 2025. For the year, advisory services revenue increased to $16.6 million, up 30% from $12.8 million in 2024.
  • The investment portfolio generated pre-tax income of $4.49 million and after-tax net income of $5.8 million for 2025, representing a return on beginning of the year shareholders’ equity of 85%.
  • For the fourth quarter, Bimini generated income of $1.52 million and, with a tax benefit of $1.87 million, net income after tax was $3.38 million.

Q&A

  • Gary Ribe, Accretive Wealth Partners, LLC: “I was just -- it looks like it's a relatively attractive price. I'm just kind of curious if you can help me understand how you guys source the deal? Were you part of a competitive bidding process? Or was this sort of an off-market deal you guys found?” Robert Cauley: “We have been working with the banker at Westir Capital, Richard Jin, and we worked to do a search. So this was not a competitive bid process. Basically, we were looking for relatively small private asset managers...They appeared to be a very attractive acquisition target for us in many respects.”
  • Ribe: “You guys won't be borrowing or anything like that from anybody to do the deal?” Cauley: “No, no borrowing to close the deal. As I mentioned, the portfolio will shrink substantially...there will be no borrowed funds whatsoever to fund the acquisition.”
  • Ribe: “In the agreement...it said something about a 30% EBITDA margin target. Is that business operating comfortably above that today?” Cauley: “I can't say yet, but that is for kind of -- there's a reference to a bonus pool. But yes, that's -- we don't envision that being a challenge, but I really can't say much beyond that.”
  • Ribe: “Do you view this as something that you can bolt advisers onto, bolt advisory firms on to? Or is this sort of a stand-alone for you?” Cauley: “Initially, it will be stand-alone. may or may not going forward, but initially, they'll be stand-alone. And they're obviously an RIA. And Bimini Advisors LLC is our RIA, which manages Orchid. So initially, they will be 2 RIAs underneath the same roof.”
  • Ribe: “I just sort of had a question on the buyback. It's nice to see that you put one in place. The stock doesn't trade a ton. If somebody had a large -- decent-sized block of shares, is there a banker that we should call to see if you guys are interested in?” Cauley: “I have a plan. So it's kind of a hands off, but the agent that we use is Ladenburg Thalmann.”
  • Ribe: “Are you guys sort of start to generate more and more cash -- is it a fair assumption that, that's just going to be redeployed to scale Royal Palm back up?” Cauley: “Yes, I would think so. That's our initial plan.”
  • Unknown Executive: “The portfolio, and we could potentially pay down some of the trust preferred debt at some point if we deem it to be attractive to do so.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst sentiment was positive, with questions focused on acquisition sourcing, capital deployment, and operational strategy, and congratulatory remarks such as “Congratulations on everything. It seems like you kind of have the wind at your back a little bit, which is nice to see.”
  • Management tone was confident and measured during prepared remarks, providing clarity on strategic decisions and directly addressing questions about deal structure, portfolio reduction, and capital use. Cauley emphasized, “there will be no borrowed funds whatsoever to fund the acquisition” and “we don't envision that being a challenge.”
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management’s tone shifted from cautious optimism regarding macroeconomic uncertainty to a more decisive and proactive approach, particularly regarding M&A and capital deployment. Analysts' tone also shifted from neutral to more positive, driven by the announced acquisition and share buyback.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance shifted from a focus on favorable Agency RMBS market conditions in Q3 2025 to a proactive diversification strategy in Q4 2025, highlighted by the planned acquisition of TJIM and reduction of the Agency RMBS portfolio.
  • Key metrics such as advisory services revenue and net income increased in Q4 versus Q3, accompanied by the launch of a share buyback program.
  • Analyst focus moved from general market conditions in Q3 to deal specifics and capital strategy in Q4.
  • Management expressed greater confidence in executing its diversification and growth strategy, while analysts responded positively to the strategic shift and capital returns.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged macroeconomic uncertainty due to geopolitical developments, stating, “the economic outlook has become very uncertain.”
  • The company is mitigating risk by funding the TJIM acquisition through cash and portfolio liquidation rather than new borrowing, and by maintaining two RIAs under one structure.
  • Analysts raised questions about deal sourcing, capital allocation, and operational integration, which management addressed directly.

Final Takeaway

Bimini Capital Management closed 2025 with strong growth in advisory services revenue and net income, driven by favorable fixed income markets and Orchid’s performance. The company is executing a major strategic shift, moving to acquire Tom Johnson Investment Management and diversify revenue streams, funded by cash and a significant reduction in its Agency RMBS portfolio. Management emphasized a disciplined approach to capital deployment, a renewed share buyback plan, and a commitment to maintaining operational stability through the transition. Investors should note the strategic evolution away from a singular focus on Agency RMBS toward a broader asset management platform, with a strong emphasis on risk management and capital efficiency as macroeconomic uncertainty intensifies.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Musk apologizes for not building xAI 'right' initially, says it's being rebuilt
data.description:
Following a mass exodus that has seen at least seven of its initial co-founders leave, including most recently Zihang Dai, Elon Musk has apologized for not building xAI (X.AI) “right.”

“xAI was not built right first time around, so is being rebuilt from the foundations up,” Musk wrote in a post on X. “The same thing happened with Tesla.” Musk followed that up with a post on Friday morning and apologized for the fact that “many talented people over the past few years were declined an offer or even an interview” with the generative artificial intelligence company. He added that he and another xAI executive, Baris Akis, are going through the company's interview history and “reaching back out to promising candidates.”

Additionally, on Friday, the Financial Times reported that Musk had ordered another round of layoffs at xAI after he grew frustrated about its coding product. He has brought in people from Tesla and SpaceX to help “audit” the start-up, the news outlet added.

Musk's comments came hours after he said he expects Digital Optimus, a joint artificial intelligence project between Tesla (TSLA) and xAI, to be ready for users to experience in six months.

Dai's departure, which was reported earlier this week, comes on the heels of the exits of other co-founders of the Grok AI chatbot maker, including Toby Pohlen, Jimmy Ba, Tony Wu, and Greg Yang.

After Dai and Zhang's exits, only two of the 11 people who started the company with Musk in 2023 — Manuel Kroiss and Ross Nordeen — remain with xAI. Last month, SpaceX (SPACE) announced it was acquiring xAI in a deal that valued the combined company at $1.25T.

In January, xAI closed a Series E funding round for $20B. The maker of the Grok chatbot did not disclose the valuation at the time, but previous reporting had indicated that the next funding round could value xAI at $230B.

Earlier this week, Tesla received clearance from the U.S. government to convert its investment in xAI into a stake in SpaceX. Tesla had previously invested $2B into xAI.

(This story has been updated with additional information regarding job cuts at xAI.)

data.title: T2 Metals boosts private financing to raise $7.6M at $0.50 per unit
data.description:
• T2 Metals (TMC) increased its non-brokered private placement financing by up to $2.6M due to strong demand.
  • The company proposed to issue up to 15.2M units at $0.50 each to raise up to $7.6M.
  • Each unit included one common share and one-half a warrant. Each full warrant entitled holders to purchase one additional share at $0.75 within two years of closing.
  • Finder’ fees may be paid on part of the financing, with net proceeds used for exploration and working capital.
  • All issued securities had a four-month hold period and required approval from the TSX Venture Exchange.
  • CEO Mark Saxon said strong interest from new and existing investors has led the Company to increase the financing, adding that its Yukon gold and silver assets are attracting attention ahead of a successful 2026.
